Title: Mutations that alter Arabidopsis flavonoid metabolism affect the circadian clock

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Phytonutrients and pharmaceuticals for human health; roles in plant development, reproduction, survival, and circadian-clock-associated physiology
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Committed-step enzyme; first step in flavonoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses p-coumaroyl-CoA with malonyl-CoA to form chalcone, initiating flavonoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for synthesis of the entire flavonoid product pool
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytoplasmic face of endoplasmic reticulum; also nucleus
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of CHS abolishes detectable flavonoid end products, alters CCA1 and TOC1 expression/promoter activity, and abolishes chlorophyll/photosynthetic rhythmicity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Null mutant analysis (tt4-11, tt4-2); RNA-seq; qRT-PCR; promoter::LUC reporter assays; hyperspectral imaging; chlorophyll quantification
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light; 12 h/12 h light-dark cycles; continuous darkness after entrainment; sucrose-containing MS medium
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: tt4-11 is a T-DNA insertion in the second exon causing loss of all detectable CHS protein and flavonoid end products; effects differed between LL and LD conditions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHS null mutants tt4-11 and tt4-2 showed altered expression of multiple circadian clock genes. 2. CHS deficiency increased CCA1-related outputs and reduced TOC1-related outputs in promoter reporter analyses. 3. Loss of CHS/flavonoids abolished rhythmic chlorophyll cycling, linking flavonoid metabolism to clock-controlled physiology.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CHS is a powerful leverage point for tuning total flavonoid content, but severe loss disrupts circadian outputs and photosynthetic rhythmicity; avoid complete knockout when engineering nutritional flavonoids and consider balanced pathway modulation instead of full pathway blockage
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Complete loss of flavonoids abolishes chlorophyll/photosynthetic rhythmicity and induces modest stress-associated transcriptome changes; possible circadian dysfunction under light conditions

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Dihydroxylated B-ring flavonoids including quercetin, cyanidin, and derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contribute to optimal circadian clock function and photosynthetic rhythmicity; flavonoids are also phytonutrients
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Dihydroxylated B-ring branch/flavonol and anthocyanin-proanthocyanidin branch
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Branch-switch enzyme for dihydroxylated flavonoid formation
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Monohydroxylated flavonoid intermediates such as kaempferol precursors
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 3'-hydroxylated/dihydroxylated flavonoids leading to quercetin, cyanidin, epicatechin derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds a 3'-hydroxyl group to the flavonoid B-ring, enabling synthesis of dihydroxylated flavonoids
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly required for formation of specific dihydroxylated flavonoid end products
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of F3'H eliminates the dihydroxyflavonoid branch, reproduces tt4-like changes in CCA1 and TOC1 promoter activity, and abolishes chlorophyll rhythmicity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Null mutant analysis (tt7-5); qRT-PCR; promoter::LUC reporter assays; hyperspectral imaging; chlorophyll quantification
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light; 12 h/12 h light-dark cycles; continuous darkness after entrainment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: tt7-5 synthesizes mono- but not dihydroxylated B-ring flavonoids; kaempferol levels are somewhat elevated and sinapate esters are unaffected; authors infer dihydroxylated flavonoids such as quercetin/cyanidin are key contributors
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The F3'H null allele tt7-5 phenocopied tt4 for altered CCA1 and TOC1 promoter activity. 2. tt7-5 also lacked photosynthetic/chlorophyll rhythmicity. 3. These data implicate dihydroxylated flavonoids, rather than CHS protein itself, in modulation of circadian function.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating F3'H can redirect flavonoid branch composition toward mono- versus dihydroxylated products, but disrupting this branch may impair circadian-linked physiology; branch engineering should preserve sufficient dihydroxylated flavonoid levels
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of dihydroxylated flavonoids abolishes chlorophyll rhythmicity and perturbs clock outputs

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Phytonutrient/pharmaceutical value and roles in plant physiology
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation via ectopic overexpression causing flavonoid hyperaccumulation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Strong activator used to generate artificially high endogenous flavonoid levels
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates flavonoid pathway gene expression leading to elevated flavonoid accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Artificially high flavonoid levels in pap1 seedlings abolished chlorophyll rhythmicity, similar to flavonoid-deficient mutants, suggesting an optimal flavonoid range for clock function
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Analysis of flavonoid-hyperaccumulating pap1 plants with chlorophyll cycling assay
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12 h/12 h light-dark entrainment followed by continuous light
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PAP1 is not the main mechanistic focus, but pap1 was experimentally used to test effects of elevated endogenous flavonoid accumulation on rhythmic physiology
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pap1 seedlings with elevated endogenous flavonoids lacked rhythmic chlorophyll cycling. 2. Both flavonoid deficiency and flavonoid excess disrupted clock output. 3. The data imply that optimal, not merely high, flavonoid abundance supports normal circadian function.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overactivation of flavonoid regulatory TFs may increase nutraceutical flavonoids but can disrupt physiological rhythmicity; dosage control is important in metabolic engineering
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flavonoid hyperaccumulation correlated with loss of chlorophyll rhythmicity
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Ectopic overexpression in the pap1 line
